coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activity of hollow mesoporous SiO2-BiOI/CQDs photocatalyst under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chenhui
Liang, Ying
Xu, Siyuan
Fu, Jiangfeng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs hybrid materials were synthesized successfully via a facile method. Afterwards, the structures, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic performances of the prepared materials were investigated by multiple techniques. This research showed that the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials demonstrated obviously improved photocatalytic performance compared with BiOI. In the photocatalysis process of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation, the optimum photocatalytic performance would be obtained when the content of BiOI and the weight percentage of CQDs was appropriate. In this photocatalytic system, the enhanced photoactivity was mainly attributed to the synergistic effects of the huge BET surface area of H-mSiO2, multiple reflections of the internal cavities of H-mSiO2, facilitation of the reactant molecules transfer, efficient e−-h+ pair separation via CQDs, and the up-converted PL property of CQDs. The radical trapping experiments revealed that O2 •−, e− and h+ were the main active species during the photocatalysis process. A possible mechanism of H-mSiO2 and CQDs for the enhancement of visible light performance was proposed.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activity of hollow mesoporous SiO2-BiOI/CQDs photocatalyst under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs hybrid materials were synthesized successfully via a facile method. Afterwards, the structures, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic performances of the prepared materials were investigated by multiple techniques. This research showed that the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials demonstrated obviously improved photocatalytic performance compared with BiOI. In the photocatalysis process of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation, the optimum photocatalytic performance would be obtained when the content of BiOI and the weight percentage of CQDs was appropriate. In this photocatalytic system, the enhanced photoactivity was mainly attributed to the synergistic effects of the huge BET surface area of H-mSiO2, multiple reflections of the internal cavities of H-mSiO2, facilitation of the reactant molecules transfer, efficient e−-h+ pair separation via CQDs, and the up-converted PL property of CQDs. The radical trapping experiments revealed that O2 •−, e− and h+ were the main active species during the photocatalysis process. A possible mechanism of H-mSiO2 and CQDs for the enhancement of visible light performance was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis has been regarded as one of the most promising techniques for solving the global energy crisis and environmental pollution issues [1–12]. In recent years, bismuth oxyhalides, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I), have attracted a lot of attention due to their unique layered structure, excellent electrical property, optical property and catalytic performance [3,13–15]. Among them, BiOI has received more attention due to its relatively narrow band gap (1.77–1.92 eV) [5]. However, the photocatalytic acticity of the pure BiOI has been limited by some inherent drawbacks, such as low efficiency of light absorption, ineffective separation of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs, low surface affinity between BiOI and organic pollutants, and so on [10]. Therefore, it is still of great importance and urgency to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOI considering future practical application.

Up to now, BiOI has been modified by a lot of materials in order to improve its photocatalytic performance, such as BiFeO3 [4], ZnSn(OH)6 [16], Bi2S3 [17], SnO2−x [5], Ag [18], MoS2 [19], BiPO4 [20], CdS [21], Ag2Mo2O7, ZnO [22], g-C3N4 [23,24], Bi2WO6 [25], Bi12TiO20 [26], TiO2 [27], (BiO)2CO3 [28], and BiOBr [29,30], etc. However, coupling these large-size materials with BiOI could hardly build perfect interacted interfaces between each other, thus the contact interfaces of these hybrid systems are insufficient and incompact. Therefore, there would be surface defects among the interfaces which could act as recombination centers of photo-generated charge carriers and limit the photocatalytic performance. Furthermore, the incomplete contact between the interface will increase the conduction resistance of electrons, so that the photoexcited electron-hole pair can not be separated effectively [10,31].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials with size less 10 nm, have attracted extensive attention due to their great potential for a wide variety of applications [32–34]. CQDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions [32], which provide the CQDs with superior electron transfer and reservoir properties [14]. Moreover, CQDs have the up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) properties, which could expand the light utilization range of nanomaterials and enhance photo-induced electron transfer [32,35]. Therefore, a series of CQDs-based photo-catalysts have been synthesized to improve the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors due to the excellent photo-induced electron transfer, electron reservoir properties and good up-converted PL behavior of CQDs, such as CQDs/ZnIn2S4 [8], CQD/BiVO4 [36,37], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [31,38], CQDs/BiOI [14], CQDs/g-C3N4 [39], CQDs/Ag3PO4 [40], CQDs/TiO2 [41–44], BiVO4/CQDs/FeVO4 [45], Co-BiOCl/CQDs [46], CQDs/g-C3N4/BiOBr [47], CQDs/ZnO [48,49], CQDs/Clay@BiOBr [34], CQDs/Bi2MoO6 [50,51], CQDs/BiOBr [34,52–54], BiOCl/CQDs/rGO [55], Fe2O3/CQDs [56], CNS/CQDs [57], CQD-SnNb2O6/BiOCl [58], CQDs/Bi7O9I3 [59], CQDs/PpPD [60], Ag2S/CQDs/CuBi2O4 [61] and so on. However, the structure–activity relationships of CQDs-based composites were still relatively insufficient and the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs-based materials need to be further investigated.

It is well known that porous and hollow solids have excellent adsorptive properties which could improve the photocatalytic performance obviously [62]. To the best of our knowledge, hollow mesoporous silica submicrospheres (H-mSiO2), which could allow multiple scattering of the irradiated light in the internal cavity, might prolong the action time of the irradiated light and promote the generation of the photo-excited electrons efficiently [63,64]. Simultaneously, the orderly mesoporous opening structure is beneficial to the transfer of reactant molecules [64]. In view of the superiority of H-mSiO2 and CQDs mentioned above, a novel H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs photocatalyst was synthesized successfully via a facile method. The relationship between the structure and property of the photocatalyst was investigated in details. The photocatalytic activity of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials were evaluated by the photodegradation of rhodamine (RhB). Furthermore, a reasonable mechanism of pollutant photodegradation in this system was proposed based on free radicals trapping experiments.

All of the reagents were analytical pure. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), absolute ethanol (EtOH), ammonia solution (25 wt%), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3), acetone, bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), 3-triethoxysilylpropylamine (APTES), aniline, potassium iodide (KI), ethylene glycol (EG), citric acid, ethylenediamine, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), AgNO3, p-benzoquinone (BQ), and rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from J & K Chemical.

Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples was recorded on a Perkin-Elmer 580BIR spectrophotometer using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Bruker AXS D8-advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kαradiation. The morphologies and sizes of the samples were characterized using a JEOL JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) datas were collected to examine the chemical states of the multi-component photocatalyst with an Axis Ultra instrument (Kratos Analytical, Manchester, U.K.) under ultrahigh vacuum condition (<10-6 Pa) and using a monochromatic Al KαX-ray source (1486.6 eV). N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were obtained on a TriStar II 20 apparatus. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to calculate the specific surface area based on the adsorption branches. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer by using BaSO4 as a reference at room temperature.

The preparation process was obtained by modifying a reported method [64] (Supplementary). The solid silica submicrospheres were prepared by the traditional StÖber hydrolysis method. Subsequently, the mesoporous silica submicrospheres with a core–shell structure were prepared by coating the silica on the surface of solid silica submicrospheres in the presence of CTAB. Then, the hollow mesoporous silica submicrospheres were prepared by selective etching under the protection of CTAB. After that, the mesoporous structure was prepared by calcination at 500 ◦C. Finally, aforementioned hollow mesoporous SiO2 was modified by the APTES at an acetonic system containing a small amount of ammonia solution (25 wt%) [63,64].

The Bi3+ ions were anchored on the surface of amino-functionalized hollow mesoporous silica microspheres by the complexation between amino groups and Bi3+ ions, then the BiOI nanophotocatalyst were successfully synthesized on the surface of the hollow mesoporous silica microspheres after the Bi3+ ions were converted to BiOI via a solvothermal synthesis. Briefly, 0.3 g of the above amino-functionalized hollow mesoporous SiO2 submicrospheres were uniformly dispersed in 80 ml of anhydrous ethanol with the aid of ultrasound, and then 1.0 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added to the system and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 1 h to make Bi3+ pass the complex effect with the amino group. The product was successfully complexed on the surface of hollow mesoporous silica. After the reaction was completed, the product was centrifuged at a rotational speed of 10,000 rpm, washed with deionized water for several times, and the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O that failed to be complexed was removed, and the powder sample was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. Then BiOI was grown on the surface of SiO2 microspheres by solvothermal method. First, the above sample and 0.7 g KI were added to 70 ml ethylene glycol, which was completely dispersed by ultrasonic action to form a yellow uniform dispersion system, and then the system was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel reactor. The reaction was continued at 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the products were centrifugally separated and collected at 10000 rpm, and washed with deionized water for 4–5 times. Finally, the hollow mesoporous H-mSiO2-BiOI photocatalyst in powder form was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. At the same time, in order to study the effect of the amount of BiOI on the catalytic performance, the dosage of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was changed, among which the dosage was 0.05g, 0.1g, 0.3g, 0.5g and 1.0g, respectively. At the same time, the effect of solvothermal reaction time on the catalytic performance was studied, and the solvothermal reaction time was 1 h, 3 h and 5 h, respectively.

In the first step, CQDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and 36.75 g citric acid and 12 ml ethylenediamine were respectively weighed and added to 350 ml deionized water, ultrasonic for about 10 min, so that they presented a uniform mixture, and then transferred to 500 ml polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel reactor. After the reaction at 200 °C for 8 h, the samples were separated and collected by dialysis and spin distillation, and the collected samples were dried by powder drying refrigerator to obtain the powder samples of CQDs. In the second step, the hollow mesoporous H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs composite material was synthesized by solvothermal method. Firstly, A certain amount of CQDs and 0.7 g KI were dissolved in 40 ml glycol, which was then ultrasound-assisted to form a uniform dispersion system and recorded as solution A. Then 0.3 g hollow mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and 0.5 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were weighed and added to 80 ml ethanol and stirred for 1 h. After the reaction was completed, they were centrifugally separated and collected at a rotational speed of 10,000 rpm. Then, they were evenly dispersed in 30 ml glycol by ultrasonic-assisted action. Form A uniform dispersion system B solution, and then, under the action of ultrasound and agitation, the A and B solution are mixed evenly, and then transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene stainless steel reactor, reaction at 180 °C for three hours, after the reaction is over, the product is separated and collected by centrifugation, and finally, Hollow mesoporous H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs multistage photocatalyst was prepared by vacuum freeze-drying method. At the same time, we studied the effect of new material CQDs on the performance of the catalyst by changing the amount of CQDs.

The photocatalytic activities of the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs photocatalyst were evaluated respectively by catalyzing the photodegradation of RhB in aqueous solution at room temperature under the visible light irradiation with one 500 W xenon lamp (CHF-XM500, lightintensity = 600 mW/cm2) located at 20 cm away from the reaction solution. All the UV lights with the wavelength less than 420 nm were removed by a glass filter (JB-420) to make sure that the photocatalytic reaction was really driven by visible-light.

Typically, 0.1 g photocatalyst was dispersed into 100 ml of RhB aqueous solution (20 mg/L). Before light irradiating, the suspension was stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach adsorption equilibrium of dye molecules on the surface of the photocatalyst. Then, 5 ml sample was extracted from the above solution and centrifuged to remove the powders, and the filtrates are analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy to determine the concentration of the RhB. Afterwards, the solution was subjected to visible light for catalytic degradation. Samples (5 ml) were taken every 5 min during the experiment and centrifuged to remove the powders. The filtrates are analyzed to determine the concentration of the dye by UV–vis spectroscopy. In this study, the photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI with different amount of BiOI was evaluated by the above-mentioned processes. Here, the dosage of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was 0.05g, 0.1g, 0.3g, 0.5g and 1.0g, respectively. At the same time, the effect of hydrothermal reaction time on H-mSiO2-BiOI performance was studied, and the hydrothermal reaction time was 1 h, 3 h and 5 h, respectively. Meanwhile, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs with the different weight percent of CQDs was assessed (CQDs = 0.1 %, 0.25 %, 0.5 %).

After experiment finished, the catalyst was collected by centrifugal separation and washed several times with ethanol and distilled water respectively, then the recovered catalyst would undergo the above-mentioned process to evaluate the stability of the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs. See (Fig. 1 ).

For detecting the active species during photocatalytic reactivity, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radical (O2 •−), electron (e−) and holes (h+) were investigated by adding 1.0 mM IPA (a scavenger of •OH) [65], 1.0 mM BQ (a scavenger of O2 •−) [10], 1.0 mM AgNO3 (a scavenger of e−) and 1.0 mM KI (a scavenger of h+), respectively. The method was similar to the former photocatalytic performance test.

The crystallographic structure of the H-mSiO2-BiOI and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs contents were investigated by XRD. The diffraction spectra were shown in Fig. 2 (a-c). Fig. 2 (a) shows that all diffraction peaks of H-mSiO2-BiOI prepared with different BiOI content are identical to the structure of the tetragonal phase BiOI (JCPDS card No. 10–0445). The diffraction peak becomes stronger with the increase of BiOI loading content.

As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the characteristic diffraction peaks under different solvothermal reaction times are almost the same, which indicates that the solvothermal reaction time will not affect the crystal structure of BiOI materials. Fig. 2 (c) shows that the characteristic diffraction peaks of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials all belong to tetranite-structured BiOI (JCPDS 10–0445), and there is no characteristic diffraction peak of CQDs at 2θ = 26°, which might be due to the low amount of CQDs.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the FT-IR characterization of the prepared materials. The peaks at 1069 cm−1 and 548 cm−1 is associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of Si-O-Si in FT-IR spectrum of H-mSiO2-NH2. Meanwhile, the peaks at 2922 cm−1 and 2849 cm−1 were attributed to δC-H symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of methyl and methylene, respectively. Moreover, the peak at 1526 cm−1 corresponds to N-H bending vibration, while the peak at 692 cm−1 corresponds to ϒN-H stretching vibration. The above results indicate that amino-functionalized H-mSiO2 microspheres were successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 3 (b), the FT-IR spectra of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQD are almost the same as the FT-IR spectrum of H-mSiO2-BiOI. This may be because the amount of CQDs is too low to detect, which was consistent with the results of the previous XRD characterization.

XPS was carried out in order to confirm the surface compositions and chemical state of the as-prepared H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the survey spectrum of the as-prepared photocatalyst, which indicates that there are Si, C, O, Bi and I elements in H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs. Fig. 4b shows the high-resolution spectra of Si. Two peaks located at 100.4 eV and 101.3 eV can be assigned to the characteristic peaks of Si-C and Si-O bonds respectively. Fig. 4c displays the high resolution XPS spectra of the C 1 s in samples. The peak at 284.6 eV could be assigned to C-C bonds, while the peak located at 286.2 eV is attributed to C-O-C bonds. Moreover, the peak at 288.4 eV could be ascribed to the C = O bond. The results demonstrates that CQDs exists in the sample. In Fig. 4d, the O 1 s peaks located at 529.9 eV and 527.3 eV are indexed to the Bi-O and Si-O, respectively. Two peaks located at 161.7 eV and 151.6 eV in Fig. 4e correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in BiOI, respectively. In Fig. 4f, the peaks of I 3d at 616.4ev and 627.9ev are the characteristic diffraction peaks of I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 in BiOI. These results indicate that H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs has been successfully prepared.

Fig. 5 shows the TEM, HRTEM images of as-prepared H-mSiO2 and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.25 % samples. As shown in Fig. 5a, H-mSiO2 microsphere has an internal cavity, and the diameter of the cavity is about 300 nm. Fig. 5b demonstrates that the H-mSiO2-BiOICQDs-0.25 % photocatalyst with an internal cavity was successfully obtained. It can be seen that there was no aggregation of BiOBr material on the surface of the H-mSiO2 microsphere. Most of the BiOBr photocatalyst were anchored onto the surface of the H-mSiO2 microsphere in the form of nanosheet structure. Simultaneously, there might be a small portion of BiOBr is attached on the surface of the H-mSiO2 microsphere in the shape of nanoparticle. In addition, the particle size of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.25 % (Fig. 5b) is found to be less than that of H-mSiO2 microsphere (Fig. 5a), which might be due to the high temperature and high pressure during solvothermal process.

Moreover, H-mSiO2-BiOICQDs-0.25 % material was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). Clear lattice fringes can be seen on the surface of H-mSiO2 microspheres (Fig. 5c), indicating the high crystallinity of BiOI, which is a typical characteristic of photocatalysts with high photocatalytic activity [63,64]. The clear lattice fringes were about 0.301 nm, which were in accordance with (102) crystallographic planes of BiOI. Furthermore, It can be observed obviously that several dark dots (CQDs) were dispersed on the surface of BiOI nanosheets, which suggested that the CQDs has been modified on the BiOI nanosheets successfully.

In order to study the influence of BiOI loading on the specific surface area and pore structure of H-mSiO2-BiOI materials, N2 adsorption/desorption was performed on a series of prepared H-mSiO2-BiOI materials with different BiOI loading. The results are shown in Table 1 . Before BiOI loading, the BET surface area of the amino functionalized H-mSiO2 was 818.99 m2/g, the single-point total pore volume was 0.6275 cm3/g, and the average BJH pore diameter was 2.68 nm. After BiOI loading, the specific surface area and single-point total pore volume of the prepared series of H-mSiO2-BiOI decreased significantly, and the average BJH pore diameter increased significantly. For example, the BET surface area, the pore volume and the average BJH pore diameter of H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.3 was 263.02 m2/g, 0.5101 cm3/g and 7.75 nm. The reason might be the morphology change of the submicrostructures after being treated at high temperature and pressure of the solvent-thermal reaction. The significant increase of the pore diameter is a primary cause for the decrease of the BET surface area. Moreover, the growth of the BiOI nanoparticles in the mesoporous pore channels might also lead to a significant reduction in the BET surface area and pore volume.

In addition, it can be seen from Table 1 that with the increase of BiOI loading, its BET surface area increases first and then decreases. The reason may be that it is difficult for a small amount of BiOI to generate lamellar structure, therefore most of the BiOI enters the pore channel in the form of nano particles, resulting in a significant decrease in BET area and pore volume. With the increase of BiOI loading, it is relatively easy to form BiOI lamellar structures on the surface of H-mSiO2. Hence, the accumulation of BiOI nano particles in the channel is reduced, so the BET surface area and pore volume are correspondingly improved. However, with the further increase of BiOI loading, a large number of lamellar structures will be formed on the surface of H-mSiO2, which may cover part of the pore structure. Thus, the specific surface area and pore volume are reduced.

In order to study the influence of solvent-thermal time on the microstructure, N2 adsorption/desorption characterization of H-mSiO2-BiOI materials prepared under different solvent-thermal time conditions was carried out. As shown in Table 2 , the BET surface area and pore volume increase first and then decrease, which might be due to the size change of the BiOI over time.Specifically, the BiOI particles generated under a short solvent-thermal time are small and easy to enter the pore. With the prolongation of solvent-thermal time, the BiOI particle size will increase correspondingly and the probability of entering the pore will be reduced. Therefore, the BET surface area and pore volume would increase. When the solvent-thermal time is extended further, the size of BiOI particle size will increase further. BiOI will accumulate on the surface of H-mSiO2, leading to decrease in BET surface area and pore volume. As a consequence, the BET surface area and pore volume of H-mSiO2-BiOI materials will first increase and then decrease with the solvent-thermal time.

N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the prepared H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials with different CQDs ratio were performed in order to research the effect of the CQDs ratio on the surface area and pore distribution of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials (Table 3 ). Table 3 demonstrates that too little or too much CQDs introduction is unfavorable to the BET surface area and pore volume. The reason might be that too little CQDs could facilitate the entry of CQDs into the pore channels of H-mSiO2-BiOI, while too much CQDs might agglomerate on the surface. Thereby, both of them would reduce the surface area and pore volume.

According to the above analysis, the introduction of BiOI/CQDs would change the BET surface area and pore structure of H-mSiO2 to some extent. However, the as-prepared H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs still have a high BET surface area due to the huge surface area of H-mSiO2. In sum, the large BET surface area and porous hollow structure of H-mSiO2 could facilitate more efficient contact of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs with organic contaminants, and may improve its photocatalytic activity effectively.
Optical absorption property is a key factor influencing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [1,5,11,59,66,67], which is usually evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS).

Fig. 7
shows the DRS of BiOI, H-mSiO2-BiOI and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs. It demonstrates that the absorption edges of H-mSiO2-BiOI materials show red shifts compared with BiOI, which means that the optical absorption in visible light is enhanced. The reason may be that the hollow structure of H-mSiO2 can reflect light continuously, thus improve the utilization of the light. After the introduction of CQDs, its absorption of visible light is significantly enhanced further, which might be due to the strong visible light responsiveness of CQDs. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs under visible light could be improved efficiently. This result corresponds with Figs. 10 and 11
.

The photoluminescence (PL) intensity could reveal the recombination rate of the photogenerated e− and h+, which are conducive to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts [24,30,31,65,68]. It is well known that the weaker intensity, the lower recombination rate of e− and h+ [42,44]. Fig. 8 shows the PL spectra of H-mSiO2-BiOI and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs. Here, the dosage of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O is 0.5 g, the reaction time is 3 h and weight percent of CQDs is 0.25 %. Fig. 8 shows that all the materials have strong emission peak centered between 370 and 400 nm with an excitation wavelength at 275 nm. As shown in Fig. 8, the PL intensity of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs are lower than that of H-mSiO2-BiOI, meaning decreasing recombination efficiency of e− and h+. This might be attributed to the efficient photo-excited carrier transfer between BiOI and CQDs, resulting in effective separation of e− and h+.

Photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs were assessed by degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The absorption–desorption balances were achieved between the catalyst and dye in 30 min before photocatalysis. Fig. 9 shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of BiOI and H-mSiO2-BiOI materials with different BiOI amounts under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 9, the saturated adsorption of H-mSiO2-BiOI photo-catalyst is significantly higher than that of pure BiOI. The saturated adsorption of BiOI, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.05 g, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.1 g, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.3 g, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g and H-mSiO2-BiOI-1.0 g are 6.67 %, 10.89 %, 5.58 %, 17.64 %, 74.75 % and 77.93 %, respectively. It indicates that the presence of the internal cavity can significantly enhance the adsorption ability of the prepared photocatalyst for the target organic pollutant, which might be caused by the concentration gradient between the internal cavity and the bulk solution.

In the process of photo-degradation, it can be seen that the direct photolysis of RhB is negligible without photocatalyst. Meanwhile, pure BiOI catalyst has no obvious photocatalytic effect on RhB also. However, the H-mSiO2-BiOI materials have a significant degradation effect on RhB. Moreover, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g material shows the best photocatalytic activity. As the BiOI loading amount increases, the photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI catalyst first increases and then decreases. On the one hand, small amount BiOI loading leads to insufficient active sites. On the other hand, excessive BiOI loading leads to obvious agglomeration on the surface of H-mSiO2. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance will be reduced accordingly.

Fig. 10 shows the degradation of RhB by H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g at different solvent thermal times under visible light. As shown in Fig. 10, the saturated adsorption of H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-1 h, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-3 h and H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-5 h for RhB are 91.34 %, 74.75 % and 42.03 %, respectively. In the subsequent photodegradation process (Fig. 10), the RhB was almost completely degraded by either H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-1 h or H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-3 h after 100 min visible light irradiation. However, nearly 22.81 % of RhB was not degraded by H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-5 h after 100 min visible light irradiation. The reason might be that the generated BiOI could agglomerate on the surface of H-mSiO2 due to the high temperature and high pressure conditions with the extension of reaction time, which is unfavorable to the photo-catalytic performance. Furthermore, in the photodegradation process, about 8.66 % and 25.25 % of RhB was photo-degraded by H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-1 h and H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-3 h, respectively. It demonstrates that the photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-3 h is better than that of H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-1 h. Therefore, H-mSiO2-BiOI-0.5 g-3 h composite photocatalyst is confirmed as the optimal photocatalyst.

Fig. 11 shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of H-mSiO2-BiOI/ CQDs photocatalysts with different amounts of CQD under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 11, the saturated adsorption capacities of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0 %, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.1 %, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.25 % and H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.5 % for RhB are 74.75 %, 48.65 %, 91.24 % and 39.30 %, respectively. During the photo-degradation process, the introduction amount of CQDs also has obvious influence on the photocatalytic performance of the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs appears a process of decline, rise and decline as the amount of CQDs introduced increases. It is obvious that the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs-0.25 % has the optimal photo-degradation efficiency for RhB. It is almost completely degraded after 40 min visible light irradiation.

In summary, the H-mSiO2-BIOI/CQDs photocatalyst has a optimal photocatalytic performance when the dosage of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O is 0.5 g, solvent thermal time is 3 h and the introduction amount of CQDs is 0.25 %, which is consistent with the previous results obtained by N2 adsorption/desorption, UV–vis spectra and PL spectra.

The photo-stability of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O = 0.5 g, t = 3 h, CQDs = 0.25 %) photocatalysts were studied by recycled experiments under visible light. After every 40 min photo-catalytic reaction, the catalyst was collected by centrifugal separation and washed several times with ethanol and distilled water, respectively. Afterwards, repeated degradation experiments were conducted on RhB. As shown in Fig. 12 , the photocatalytic performance of the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs hardly decreased after five recycles, demonstrating excellent photostability.

Furthermore, the recovered H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O = 0.5 g, t = 3 h, CQDs = 0.25 %) after five cycles was characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 13 , XRD spectrum of the regenerated H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs has no obvious change compared with the initial photocatalyst. Therefore, it is further indicated that H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs catalyst has excellent photo-stability and bright prospects in the photo-degradation of organic pollutants.

As shown in Fig. 14, the degradation efficiency is reduced significantly when IPA (•OH scavenger) is added to the degradation system, indicating that •OH radicals play a key role in the degradation process. In addition, the degradation efficiency is also reduced obviously when BQ (O2 •− scavenger) and KI (h+ scavenger) are added to the degradation system, so O2 •− and h+ play an important role in the degradation process. However, photodegradation was completed at 40 min illumination when AgNO3 (e− scavenger) was added into the degradation system, demonstrating that e− does not have direct effect on the photocatalytic process.

A possible photocatalytic mechanism diagram of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs photocatalyst was proposed in Fig. 15 according to the experimental results above. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs could be attributed to the several reasons as follows.

Firstly, huge BET surface area of H-mSiO2 improves the photocatalytic performance of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs effectively. It has been widely recognized that a high BET surface area is advantageous for absorb more active species and reactants on its surface, which thus favors the improvement of photocatalytic performance [31,64]. Secondly, the existence of the internal cavities of this novel photocatalyst would lead to multiple reflections of the irradiated light and enhance the utilization of light efficiently, which is very beneficial to the enhancement of the photo-activity of this materials [62–64]. Simultaneously, the orderly mesoporous opening structure can effectively facilitate the transfer of reactant molecules. Thirdly, when the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs is irradiated by visible light, the e− can be excited from the VB of BiOI to the CB, leaving the h+ on the VB. The photo-generated e− would transfer from the CB of BiOI to CQDs. Because the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs made it easier to transfer the photo-generated e− by the interface between BiOI and CQDs, resulting in efficient e−-h+ pair separation and thus an enhanced photocatalytic activity. Fourthly, the up-converted PL property of CQDs could convert longer wavelength light to shorter wavelength light, which can in turn excite BiOI to form photo-excited e−-h+ pairs [32]. Thus, more e−-h+ pairs are excited, result in an enhanced photo-catalytic performance. Afterwards, the photo-excited e− can be captured by O2 molecules in the system to generate the O2 •− radicals. Meanwhile, the h+ on the VB of BiOI could oxidize the OH− to produce •OH. As a result, the generated •OH radical, O2 •− radical and the h+ would play important role in the photo-degradation process, leading to dramatic photo-catalytic performance. In a word, the excellent photocatalytic activity of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs could be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the huge BET surface area of H-mSiO2, multiple reflections of the internal cavities of H-mSiO2, facilitation of the reactant molecules transfer, efficient e−-h+ pair separation via CQDs, and the up-converted PL property of CQDs.

In summary, a novel H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs hybrid material with excellent photocatalytic performance was successfully prepared through a facile method. Compared with the pure BiOI, H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs hybrid material demonstrated significantly improved photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O = 0.5 g, t = 3 h, CQDs = 0.25 %) photocatalysts exhibited the optimal photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB. In addition, the H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs materials showed outstanding stability. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of H-mSiO2-BiOI/CQDs could be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the huge BET surface area of H-mSiO2, multiple reflections of the internal cavities of H-mSiO2, facilitation of the reactant molecules transfer, efficient e−-h+ pair separation via CQDs, and the up-converted PL property of CQDs. The radicals trapping experiments revealed that O2 •−, e− and h+ were the main active species during the photocatalysis process. This work is expected to provide important information and encourage new design of H-mSiO2-based or CQDs-based hybrid materials with high photocatalytic performance under visible light.
